The phrase 'fēishī, dùnshī, fēngshī, chénshī, shīzhù, guǐjī' (飛尸遁尸，風尸沉尸，尸注鬼擊) refers to a classical nosological category of 'possession' or 'miasmic' illnesses attributed to wandering spectral influences (尸, lit. 'corpse-spirits'); these have no direct modern clinical equivalents and are rendered descriptively. 2. The alchemical note about the juice subduing sulfur and processing mercury (伏硫製汞) reflects the Daoist alchemical context of the Tǔsù tradition. 3. 'Inner Compiler' (內翰) is a court title; Hong Mai and Shen Kuo are both well-known Song dynasty literati whose formularies are cited here. 4. The phrase 氣下 in 'stop downward movement of qi' (止氣下) may refer to qi desertion, flatulence, or prolapse — the exact pathological sense is context-dependent in classical Chinese medicine. 5. OCR had 氣下 with a possible missing character (止氣下澼 or similar), but the text as it stands is coherent and has been translated as given.

results.title_inferred: Gan Teng (Sweet Vine) – Properties, Uses, and Preparations
results.original_text:
【釋名】甜藤（《嘉祐》）、感藤。

時珍曰︰甘、感音相近也。又有甜藤、甘露藤，皆此類，並附之。忍冬一名甜藤，與此不同。

【集解】藏器曰︰生江南山谷。其藤大如雞卵，狀如木防己。斫斷吹之，氣出一頭。其汁甘美如蜜。

汁

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】調中益氣，通血氣，解諸熱，止渴（藏器）。除煩悶，利五臟，治腎釣氣。其葉研敷蛇蟲咬（大明）。解熱痢及膝腫（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**
Alternative names: Tian Teng [Sweet Vine] (from the *Jiayou Bencao*), and Gan Teng [Feeling/Sensitive Vine].

Li Shizhen notes: The pronunciations of 甘 (gān, sweet) and 感 (gǎn, feeling) are similar, hence the alternate name. There are also plants called Tian Teng [Sweet Vine] and Ganluteng [Sweet-Dew Vine], which belong to the same category and are appended here. Note that Rěndōng (Lonicera japonica, honeysuckle) is also sometimes called Tian Teng [Sweet Vine], but that is a different plant.

**Collected Explanations**
Chen Cangqi states: It grows in mountain valleys of Jiangnan [south of the Yangtze River]. The vine is as thick as a chicken's egg and resembles [the stem of] Mu Fangji [*Cocculus trilobus* or similar Menispermaceae]. When cut and blown into from one end, air passes through to the other end. Its sap is sweet and delicious, like honey.

**Sap**

*Flavor and Nature:* Sweet, balanced [neutral in temperature], non-toxic.

*Indications and Treatments:*
- Harmonizes the middle [digestive system] and replenishes qi, promotes circulation of blood and qi, resolves all types of heat, and quenches thirst. (Chen Cangqi)
- Eliminates vexation and oppression, benefits the five viscera [wǔzàng], and treats kidney colic [contracted qi of the kidneys]. The leaves, ground up and applied topically, treat snake and insect bites. (Da Ming [Ri Huazi])
- Resolves dysentery due to heat and swelling of the knees. (Li Shizhen)

entities_mentioned.name: 甘藤 (Gān Téng / Sweet Vine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Sabia japonica or a species of Menispermaceae; some scholars suggest Calamus or a related liana; identity not firmly established
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a thick vine growing in Jiangnan mountain valleys, with hollow internals allowing air to pass through when cut, and producing honey-sweet sap. These features are suggestive of certain lianas but no confident modern identification can be made.

entities_mentioned.name: 甜藤 (Tián Téng / Sweet Vine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as an alternate name for 甘藤, and also as a separate related plant appended to this entry. Li Shizhen explicitly distinguishes it from the honeysuckle (Lonicera japonica), which shares the same common name.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘露藤 (Gānlù Téng / Sweet-Dew Vine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a related plant of the same category, appended to this entry without further elaboration.

entities_mentioned.name: 忍冬 (Rěndōng / Honeysuckle)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lonicera japonica
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned only to clarify that its alternate name Tian Teng (Sweet Vine) should not be confused with the 甘藤 of this entry.

entities_mentioned.name: 木防己 (Mù Fángjǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cocculus trilobus or Sinomenium acutum (Menispermaceae family)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological reference point for the appearance of the 甘藤 vine.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text abbreviates '《嘉祐》' (Jiayou Bencao, a Song dynasty materia medica) as '《嘉 》' — this is a common OCR truncation and has been silently restored.
2. '腎釣氣' is an unusual term; 釣 here likely means 'contracted' or 'pulling' pain (i.e., colic or spasmodic pain originating from the kidneys), rendered as 'kidney colic' for readability.
3. Attribution markers in parentheses (藏器, 大明, 時珍) refer to Chen Cangqi (*Bencao Shiyi*), Ri Huazi (*Rihua Zibencao*), and Li Shizhen himself, respectively.

results.original_text:
（《拾遺》）

藏器曰︰生江南山林下，蔓如葛。味甘，寒，無毒。主去熱煩解毒，調中氣，令人肥健。搗汁和米粉，作糗餌食，甜美，止泄。又治剝馬血毒入肉，及狂犬、牛、馬熱黃。敷蛇咬瘡。又有小葉尖長，氣辛臭者，搗敷小兒腹，除痞滿閃癖。
results.translation:
([From] the 'Bencao Shiyi' [Supplement to the Materia Medica])

Zangqi [Chen Zangqi] states: It grows beneath the mountain forests of Jiangnan, and its vine resembles that of the kudzu [Pueraria]. The flavor is sweet, the nature is cold, and it is non-toxic. It is primarily used to dispel heat and vexation [restlessness due to heat], resolve toxins, regulate the middle qi [digestive function], and make the person robust and healthy. The juice is pounded out and mixed with rice flour to make dried cakes [qiu er, a type of dried grain food] for eating — it is sweet and pleasant, and stops diarrhea. It also treats cases where the blood toxin from skinning a horse has entered the flesh, as well as jaundice [re huang, 'heat-yellow'] caused by mad dogs, cattle, or horses [i.e., from bites or contact]. It is applied topically to snake-bite sores. There is also a variety with small, pointed, and elongated leaves that has an acrid and foul smell; this variety is pounded and applied to the abdomen of young children to dispel abdominal masses [pi man], accumulations, and flank-region congealed stagnation [shan pi].

entities_mentioned.name: 甜藤 (Tian Teng, 'Sweet Vine')
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Calopogonium mucunoides, Falopia multiflora var., or more likely Gymnema sylvestre or a species of Smilax or Derris; some scholars suggest Dregea sinensis or a Rubus species. The description of a sweet-juiced, kudzu-like vine in Jiangnan forests is broadly consistent with several Fabaceae or related lianas.
entities_mentioned.notes: The entry describes two distinct forms: one sweet-tasting used in food and medicine, and one with small pointed leaves that is acrid and malodorous, used topically for children's abdominal complaints. These may represent two different taxa grouped under one name. Chen Zangqi is the Tang-dynasty author of the 'Bencao Shiyi' (c. 741 CE).

entities_mentioned.name: 糗餌 (qiu er, dried grain cakes)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical term for dried or parched grain-based food, used here to describe the preparation of the plant's juice mixed with rice flour.

results.translator_notes: The phrase '剝馬血毒入肉' refers to blood toxicity entering the body through a wound incurred while skinning a horse, a recognized occupational hazard in classical Chinese medicine. '狂犬、牛、馬熱黃' likely refers to jaundice or febrile yellowing conditions caused by bites or contact with rabid dogs, cattle, or horses. '閃癖' (shan pi) refers to a flank accumulation or stagnation disorder in children, likely corresponding to hepatosplenomegaly or similar abdominal masses in modern terms. The 'small-leafed acrid variety' described at the end may be a genuinely different plant species conflated under the same common name.

results.title_inferred: Water-Containing Vine (含水藤) — Vine Water as Medicine
results.original_text:
【釋名】大瓠藤

【集解】曰︰按︰劉欣期《交州記》云︰含水藤生嶺南及諸海邊山谷。狀若葛，葉似枸杞。多在路旁，行人乏水處便吃此藤，故以爲名。

藏器曰︰越南、朱崖、儋耳無水處，皆種大瓠藤，取汁用之。藤狀如瓠，斷之水出，飲之清美。

時珍曰︰顧微《廣州記》云︰水藤，去地一丈，斷之更生，根至地水不絕。山行口渴，斷取汁飲之。陳氏所謂大瓠藤，蓋即此物也。

藤中水

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。藏器曰︰寒。

【主治】解煩渴心燥，瘴癘丹石發動，亦宜服之（李珣）。止渴，潤五臟，去濕痺，天行時氣，利小便。其葉搗，敷中水爛瘡皮皸（藏器）。治人體有損痛，沐發令長（時珍。《廣州記》）。
results.translation:
**Names:** Also called Dà Hù Téng [Great Gourd Vine].

**Collected Explanations:**
[Anonymous/earlier source] states: According to Liu Xinqi's *Jiaozhou Ji* [Record of Jiaozhou]: the Water-Containing Vine grows in Lingnan [south of the Nanling Mountains] and in the mountain valleys near the various coastal regions. In form it resembles kudzu [Pueraria], and its leaves are similar to those of the wolfberry [Lycium]. It is commonly found along roadsides, and travelers in places where water is scarce drink from this vine — hence the name [含水藤, lit. 'water-holding vine'].

Chen Zangqi states: In regions of Jiaozhi [northern Vietnam], Zhuyai, and Dan'er [both in Hainan] where fresh water is unavailable, people all plant the Great Gourd Vine and extract its juice for use. The vine resembles a gourd in form; when cut, water flows out, and the water is clear and pleasant to drink.

Li Shizhen states: Gu Wei's *Guangzhou Ji* [Record of Guangzhou] records: The Water Vine, when cut at a height of one zhang [~3 m] above the ground, regenerates, and water flows uninterruptedly from its roots all the way down to the ground. Travelers in the mountains who are thirsty cut it and drink the juice. What Chen [Zangqi] calls the Great Gourd Vine is most likely this same plant.

---
**Vine Water** [the juice extracted from the vine]

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet, balanced [平], non-toxic. Chen Zangqi says: Cold.

**Indications and Treatments:**
- Relieves vexing thirst and heat-dryness of the heart; also suitable for consumption when miasmic pestilence [瘴癘] or adverse reactions to mineral elixirs [丹石發動] occur. (Li Xun)
- Stops thirst, moistens the five viscera [五臟], dispels damp impediment [濕痺], treats seasonal epidemic qi [天行時氣], and promotes urination. The leaves, when pounded and applied as a poultice, treat waterlogged festering sores and cracked skin [皮皸]. (Chen Zangqi)
- Treats bodily pain from injury; when used to wash the hair, promotes hair growth. (Li Shizhen, citing *Guangzhou Ji*)

entities_mentioned.name: 含水藤 (Hán Shuǐ Téng) / 大瓠藤 (Dà Hù Téng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Entada phaseoloides (Fabaceae) or a related liana known for holding water in its stem; some scholars suggest Cissus or Tetrastigma spp. (Vitaceae). Identification remains uncertain.
entities_mentioned.notes: The text emphasizes its water-storing stem used as an emergency water source by travelers in southern China and northern Vietnam. The description of the stem yielding clear drinkable water when cut is consistent with several large tropical lianas.

entities_mentioned.name: 枸杞 (Gǒuqǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium chinense or Lycium barbarum (wolfberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used only as a reference for the shape of the leaves of 含水藤.

entities_mentioned.name: 葛 (Gé)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pueraria montana var. lobata (kudzu)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used only as a reference for the overall form of the vine.

results.translator_notes: 1. The original text has '朱 ' with an apparent lacuna or OCR dropout; this has been restored to '朱崖' (Zhuyai, an ancient name for part of Hainan Island), which is the standard pairing with 儋耳 in classical texts. 2. '李珣' (Li Xun) is the probable author attribution for the first treatment entry, corrected from the abbreviated '李' in the source. 3. '丹石發動' refers specifically to toxic or adverse reactions caused by ingesting Daoist mineral/alchemical elixirs (丹石), a recognized clinical concern in Tang–Song era medicine. 4. The passage cites three sources: Liu Xinqi's *Jiaozhou Ji*, Chen Zangqi's *Bencao Shiyi*, and Gu Wei's *Guangzhou Ji*, reflecting Li Shizhen's comparative editorial method.

entities_mentioned.name: 麻黃 (Máhuáng)

entities_mentioned.name: 蘇頌 (Sū Sòng)
entities_mentioned.notes: Song Dynasty polymath and official (1020–1101), author of the Bencao Tujing; cited here as the source for the abortifacient usage.

results.translator_notes: The source text is incomplete in two places: (1) the 集解 section is cut off mid-sentence after describing the leaves ('四' likely begins a description of the four seasons or a four-directional spread of the vine); (2) the 主治 section is cut off after '治心腹', omitting the specific abdominal conditions treated. These truncations appear to be artifact of the segment boundary, not OCR damage. The condition 風勞 (wind-fatigue) refers to a classical pathological category combining wind-pathogen invasion with chronic deficiency and fatigue. The abortifacient use (墮胎氣) is clinically significant given that Aristolochia species contain aristolochic acids, which are known nephrotoxins and carcinogens.

**Names:** [Alternate name:] Shān Gāncǎo (Mountain Sweetherb).

**Collected Commentaries:** Su Song states: It grows in the mountains of Fuzhou. In early spring it puts forth solitary leaves of green color, which fall away by winter. Its vine resembles a dried twig. The bark is harvested and dried in the sun.

**Qi and Flavor:** [Entry missing / not recorded.]

**Indications and Treatments:** [For] kidney qi disorders in men (Su Song). Disperses stasis blood from injuries and trauma. Pounded and applied topically to malignant sores [惡瘡] and swollen toxins [腫毒] (Li Shizhen).

entities_mentioned.name: 紫金藤 (Zijin Teng / Purple Gold Vine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Mucuna or a related Fabaceae liana, or alternatively Salacia chinensis or a Melodinus sp.; the Fuzhou provenance and bark usage suggest a candidate in the genus Melodinus (Apocynaceae) or Celastrus, but identification remains uncertain.
entities_mentioned.notes: The alternate name 山甘草 (Mountain Sweetherb) is also applied to other plants in Chinese botanical literature, complicating identification. The description of vine-like habit with bark used medicinally and leaves shed in winter is consistent with several woody climbers native to Fujian province.

entities_mentioned.name: 山甘草 (Shān Gāncǎo / Mountain Sweetherb)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: null
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as an alternate name for 紫金藤; may correspond to different plants in different regional traditions.

results.translator_notes: The 'Qi and Flavor' [氣味] section is explicitly absent from the source text (缺, meaning 'lacking' or 'not recorded'), indicating the compiler had no information on its taste or thermal nature. Su Song (蘇頌, 1020–1101) is cited for the botanical description and the kidney-qi indication; Li Shizhen (時珍) adds the indications for traumatic blood stasis and external application to sores. The term 丈夫腎氣 ('kidney qi of men') likely refers to conditions such as impotence, seminal weakness, or lower back deficiency associated with male reproductive health in classical Chinese medicine.

results.original_text:
【釋名】石南藤（《圖經》）、丁公藤（《開寶》）、丁公寄（《別錄》）、丁父（《別錄》）、風藤。

志曰︰生依南樹，故號南藤。

藏器曰︰丁公寄，即丁公藤也。始因丁公用有效，因以得名。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰丁公寄生石間，蔓延木上。葉細，大枝赤莖，母大如磧黃有汁。七月七日采。

頌曰︰南藤，即丁公藤也。生南山山谷，今泉州、榮州有之。生依南木，莖如馬鞭，有節紫褐色，葉如杏葉而尖。采無時。又曰︰天台石南藤，四時不凋。土人采葉，治腰痛。

時珍曰︰今江南、湖南諸大山有之。細藤圓膩，紫綠色，一節一葉。葉深綠色，似杏葉而微短濃。其莖貼樹處，有小紫瘤疣，中有小孔。四時不凋，莖葉皆臭而極辣。白花蛇食其葉。

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。《別錄》曰︰甘。

【主治】金瘡痛。延年（《別錄》）。主風血，補衰老，起陽，強腰腳，除痺，變白，逐冷氣，排風邪。煮汁服，冬月浸酒服（藏器）。煮汁服，治上氣咳嗽（時珍）。

【發明】志曰︰按︰《南史》云︰解叔謙，雁門人。母有疾，夜禱，聞空中語云︰得丁公藤治之即瘥。訪醫及本草皆無此藥。至宜都山中，見一翁伐木，云是丁公藤，療風。乃拜泣求。翁並示以漬酒法。受畢，失翁所在。母服之遂愈也。

There is no fixed season for harvesting.

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。

results.translation:
**Names:** Chì Téng [Red Vine] (Bencao Gangmu), Hóng Téng [Red Vine] (Bencao Gangmu).

**Collected Explanations:** [Chen] Cangqi states: It grows in the deep mountains of southern regions. Its bark is red, as thick as a finger, and is suitable for binding objects; it peels off in strips of its own accord.

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter; balanced [neutral]; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatment:** For roundworms [ascaris], boil the vine and drink the decoction. For toothache, pound it and hold the pieces in the mouth. Boil it into a gruel and feed it to dogs to expel worms (Cangqi). It also treats all manner of wind ailments [fengbing] and unblocks the five types of urinary obstruction [wulin].

**Elaboration:** Li Shizhen states: Red Vine is excellent at killing parasites and promoting urination. Hong Mai's Yijian Zhi records the following account: Zhao Zishan suffered from tapeworm disease [cunbai chong, i.e., taeniasis]. His physician ordered him to abstain from alcohol, but he was by nature deeply fond of drinking. One day, while lodging at Tianwang Temple in Shaowu, he returned drunk in the middle of the night, desperately thirsty. He noticed a jar of water sitting in the corridor, gleaming and clear in the moonlight, and drank cup after cup of it — it was as sweet as syrup. By dawn, worms had emerged and covered an entire mat; his chest and abdomen immediately felt at ease, and the chronic ailment was completely cured. Everyone was astonished. Upon examining the water he had drunk, they found it was water in which the temple servants had been soaking red vine root [hongteng gen] in order to weave straw sandals.

entities_mentioned.name: 省藤 / 赤藤 / 紅藤 (Sheng Teng / Chi Teng / Hong Teng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Probably Calamus thysanolepis or a related Calamus species (rattan palm, Arecaceae); alternatively sometimes identified with Sargentodoxa cuneata (large-leaved sargentodoxa, Lardizabalaceae), which is also called 大血藤 or 紅藤 in later usage
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — red bark, finger-thick, used for binding, peeling in strips — fits rattan (Calamus spp.) well. However, 紅藤 in later Chinese medicine more commonly refers to Sargentodoxa cuneata. The antiparasitic and diuretic uses are consistent with both identifications. Context here favors a rattan-type plant given the description of it being used to weave sandals.

entities_mentioned.name: 寸白蟲 (cunbai chong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tapeworm (Taenia spp.), taeniasis
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Chinese term for tapeworm segments visible in stool; literally 'inch-long white worms.'

entities_mentioned.name: 蛔蟲 (huichong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ascaris lumbricoides (roundworm)

entities_mentioned.name: 五淋 (wulin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Five categories of urinary obstruction/strangury in classical Chinese medicine (stone, qi, blood, fat, and labor strangury)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical diagnostic category, not a single modern disease.

results.translator_notes: The character following '去' in the 主治 section appears incomplete or corrupted in the source OCR (rendered as just '（藏器）' after a lone '去'). From context — antiparasitic use, feeding to dogs — the missing character is almost certainly '蟲' (worms/parasites), so '去蟲' has been silently restored. The anecdote from Hong Mai's Yijian Zhi (Record of the Listener, 12th c. Song dynasty) is a standard illustrative narrative (biji) used by Li Shizhen to document empirical cures; it functions here as clinical evidence for the antiparasitic efficacy of red vine root. The preparation described — soaking root in water — is an informal cold-water maceration rather than a formal decoction.

results.translation:
【Collected Explanations】Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: The vine's bark adheres to the tree, with layered bark from the core outward. In the fourth month [lunar], it produces charming purple flowers. The people of Chang'an also cultivate it to adorn garden pools. In Jiangdong it is called 'Zhaodou vine' [招豆藤, 'beckoning bean vine']. Its seeds form pods; the kernels within the pods, when roasted until fragrant and placed into wine, prevent the wine from spoiling. Used in already-spoiled wine, they can also restore it. When its flowers are crushed and rubbed onto wine or vinegar that has turned white and deteriorated [i.e., formed mold or scum], they correct the spoilage.

【Qi and Flavor】Sweet, slightly warm, mildly toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】Prepared as a decoction resembling syrup and taken orally, it promotes the downward movement of water [diuresis/drainage]. It principally treats water-accumulation disease [edema/dropsy] (Chen Cangqi).

entities_mentioned.name: 紫藤 (Zǐténg)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wisteria sinensis (Chinese wisteria) or Wisteria floribunda
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called 招豆藤 (Zhaodou vine) in Jiangdong. A climbing vine with layered bark, producing purple flowers in spring. Its seeds and pods are noted for preserving wine and correcting spoiled wine and vinegar.

entities_mentioned.name: 水病 (shuǐ bìng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Edema / dropsy (fluid accumulation disorders)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Chinese medical category referring to abnormal accumulation of water/fluid in the body; treated here by taking a syrup-like decoction of wisteria to promote drainage.

results.translator_notes: The phrase '其花碎，拭酒醋白腐壞' is slightly ambiguous — it may mean that crushed flowers are used to wipe or treat wine/vinegar that has developed white surface spoilage (mold or acetobacter films). The 'small toxicity' (有小毒) note is significant and consistent with modern knowledge that wisteria seeds and pods contain toxic glycosides (wisterin/lectins). Chen Cangqi (陳藏器) is the Tang dynasty pharmacologist whose work Bencao Shiyi (本草拾遺) is the cited source here.

【釋名】曰︰藤蘿高丈餘，雁過皆綴其中，或云雁銜至代州雁門而生，以此為名。

【集解】曰︰按︰徐表《南州記》云︰落雁木生南海山野中。蔓生，四邊如刀削。代州雁門亦有之，蜀中雅州亦有。

頌曰︰雅州出者，苗作蔓纏繞大木，苗葉形色大都似茶，無花實。彼人四月採苗，入藥用。

莖葉

【氣味】甘，平、溫，無毒。

【主治】風痛傷折，腳氣腫，腹滿虛脹。以木皮同煮汁洗之，立效。又婦人陰瘡浮泡，以椿木皮同煮汁洗之（李珣）。產後血氣痛，並折傷內損諸疾，煮汁服（蘇頌）。
results.translation:
**Explanation of Names:** It is said that the vine grows to over one zhang [approx. 3 m] in height, and wild geese passing by become entangled within it; alternatively, it is said that geese carried it to Daizhou's Yanmen [Wild Goose Gate Pass], where it took root and grew — hence the name [Luòyàn Mù, 'Goose-alighting Wood'].

**Collected Commentaries:** According to Xu Biao's *Nanzhou Ji* [Record of the Southern Regions]: Luòyàn Wood grows in the mountain wilds of the South Sea region. It is a climbing plant, with four sides [of the stem] as if cut by a knife. It is also found at Yanmen in Daizhou, and likewise in Yazhou within Shu [Sichuan].

Su Song states: The variety produced in Yazhou sends up twining shoots that wind around large trees. The shoots and leaves in form and color greatly resemble those of tea [Camellia sinensis], but it bears no flowers or fruit. The local people gather the shoots in the fourth month for medicinal use.

**Stems and Leaves**

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet; balanced [ping], warm; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:**
- Wind-pain [rheumatic pain], fractures and injuries, leg-qi [beriberi / lower-limb swelling], abdominal fullness and vacuous distension: decoct together with tree bark [of the same plant] and wash the affected area with the liquid — effective immediately.
- Women's genital sores and blistering: decoct together with ailanthus bark [椿木皮, *Ailanthus altissima* bark] and wash with the liquid. (Li Xun)
- Postpartum blood and qi pain, as well as all conditions involving internal injury from fractures and trauma: decoct and take the liquid orally. (Su Song)

entities_mentioned.name: 落雁木 (Luòyàn Mù)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a climbing vine of uncertain identity; sometimes tentatively associated with species in Vitaceae or Menispermaceae, but no firm consensus
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — a high-climbing vine with stems having four sharp edges and leaves resembling those of tea, no flowers or fruit observed, growing in South China and Sichuan — does not map cleanly onto a confirmed modern taxon. Some sources speculatively link it to Piper or Tetrastigma species.

entities_mentioned.name: 椿木皮 (Chūn mù pí, ailanthus bark)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ailanthus altissima (Tree of Heaven) bark, or possibly Toona sinensis (Chinese toon) bark — the character 椿 can refer to either
entities_mentioned.notes: In Ming-era usage 椿 often refers to Toona sinensis, but Ailanthus altissima (臭椿) is also a common referent. Used here as a co-decoction agent for washing genital sores.

entities_mentioned.name: 徐表《南州記》(Xu Biao, Nanzhou Ji)
entities_mentioned.notes: A now largely lost early medieval Chinese geographic/natural history text on the southern regions, cited here as a botanical source.

results.translator_notes: The attribution '李珣' (Li Xun) is inferred from '李' in the source text, which appears to be a truncated citation; Li Xun (fl. 10th c.) authored the *Haiyao Bencao* and is frequently cited in the Bencao Gangmu in this abbreviated form. The phrase '以木皮同煮汁洗之' in the first treatment is ambiguous as to which tree's bark is meant — it may refer to the bark of Luòyàn Wood itself, whereas the second treatment specifies 椿木皮 (ailanthus/toon bark) explicitly. OCR rendered '李珣' as '李 ' with a space; corrected silently.

【集解】藏器曰︰千里及，藤生道旁籬落間，葉細而濃。宣湖間有之。

頌曰︰千里急，生天台山中。春生苗，秋有花。土人采花葉入眼藥。又筠州有千里光，生淺山及路旁。葉似菊葉而長，背有毛。枝幹圓而青。春生苗，秋有黃花，不結實。采莖葉入眼藥，名黃花演。蓋一物也。

【氣味】苦，平，有小毒。頌曰︰苦、甘，寒，無毒。

【主治】天下疫氣結黃，瘴瘧蠱毒，煮汁服，取吐下。亦搗敷蛇犬咬（藏器）。同甘草煮汁飲，退熱明目，不入衆藥（蘇頌）。同小青煎服，治赤痢腹痛（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Collected Explanations**

Chen Cangqi [藏器] states: Qianli-ji [千里及, 'Reaches a Thousand Li'] grows as a vine along roadsides and among hedgerows, with small but thick leaves. It is found in the Xuan and Hu [Xuanzhou and Huzhou] regions.

Su Song [頌] states: Qianli-ji [here written 千里急] grows in the Tiantai Mountains. Its seedlings emerge in spring and it flowers in autumn. Local people collect the flowers and leaves for use in eye medicines. In addition, Junzhou has a plant called Qianliguang [千里光, 'Thousand-Li Radiance'], which grows on low hills and along roadsides. Its leaves resemble chrysanthemum leaves but are longer, with hair on the underside. The stems and branches are round and green. Seedlings appear in spring; yellow flowers appear in autumn and it does not set fruit. The stems and leaves are collected for eye medicines under the name Huanghuayan [黃花演, 'Yellow-Flower演']. These are all the same plant.

**Flavor and Nature**

Bitter, balanced [平], slightly toxic. Su Song states: bitter and sweet, cold, non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments**

- For epidemic qi [疫氣] causing jaundice and binding [結黃], miasmic malaria [瘴瘧], and gu-poison [蠱毒]: decoct and drink the juice to induce vomiting and purging. Also pound and apply topically for snake and dog bites. (Chen Cangqi)
- Decocted together with licorice [甘草] and drunk as a beverage: reduces fever and brightens the eyes; not combined with other medicines in compound formulas. (Su Song)
- Decocted together with Xiaoqing [小青] and taken orally: treats dysentery with blood [赤痢] and abdominal pain. (Li Shizhen)

entities_mentioned.name: 千里及 / 千里急 / 千里光 (Qianli-ji / Qianliguang; also called 黃花演 Huanghuayan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Senecio scandens Buch.-Ham. ex D.Don (climbing groundsel / 千里光)
entities_mentioned.notes: The segment's heading uses 風延母, an alternate or regional name for the same plant. The text discusses three closely related or synonymous names — 千里及, 千里急, and 千里光 — and explicitly concludes they refer to one plant. Senecio scandens is the standard modern botanical identification for 千里光, widely used in traditional Chinese medicine for eye conditions and inflammation.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 (Gancao, licorice root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. or related Glycyrrhiza spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here in combination decoction to reduce fever and brighten the eyes.

entities_mentioned.name: 小青 (Xiaoqing)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Isatis indigotica Fort. (small indigo) or Baphicacanthus cusia (Nees) Bremek.; identification debated
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with Qianliguang to treat bloody dysentery and abdominal pain. The exact botanical identity of 小青 is contested in the literature.

results.translator_notes: The segment heading 風延母 does not appear in the body text but is the chapter-level entry name given for this drug in the Bencao Gangmu; the body text uses the more common drug names 千里及/千里光. The term 黃花演 is unusual — 演 may be a variant or phonetic character without a clear standard meaning in this context, possibly a local name or OCR/print variant. The phrase 不入衆藥 ('not combined with other medicines') is a formulaic caution indicating this drug should be used as a simple (standalone) decoction rather than in compound prescriptions.

【釋名】樹名海藤。

曰︰按︰郭義恭《廣志》云︰出岳、鄂等州諸山崖。樹名海藤。花有蕊，散落石上，彼人收之，謂之沙黃。就樹采者輕妙，謂之臘黃。今人訛爲銅黃，銅、藤音謬也。此與石淚采之無異。畫家及丹灶家時用之。

時珍曰︰今畫家所用藤黃，皆經煎煉成者，舐之麻人。按︰周達觀《真臘記》云︰國有畫黃，乃樹脂。番人以刀斫樹枝滴下，次年收之。似與郭氏說微不同，不知即一物否也？

**Nomenclature:** Also known as Hucai [蔛菜] (as cited by Su Gong), and Hurong [蔛榮].

**Collected Explanations:** Su Gong states: Hucai is found in many places, growing beside water. Its leaves resemble those of zexie [Alisma plantago-aquatica] but are smaller. The flowers are a blue-white [greenish-white] color. It is also suitable for steaming and eating; people in Jiangnan use it steamed with fish, and it is exceptionally delicious. The stems are gathered in the fifth and sixth months [lunar calendar] and dried in the sun for use.

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet, cold, non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:** Sudden [acute] fever with labored breathing; erysipelas and swelling [dan swelling, 丹腫] in children. (Su Gong)

results.translator_notes: The term 丹腫 (dan zhong) refers to erysipelas-like inflammatory swellings with redness, a classical pediatric condition. '暴熱喘息' describes acute-onset fever accompanied by respiratory distress. Su Gong (蘇恭) is the Tang-dynasty pharmacologist Su Jing, compiler of the Xinxiu Bencao (659 CE), whose commentary is cited throughout the Bencao Gangmu. The fifth and sixth months refer to the traditional lunar calendar, roughly June–July in the Gregorian calendar.

時珍曰︰薞蕪，乃酸模之音轉，酸模又酸母之轉，皆以味而名，與三葉酸母草同名。掌禹錫以薞蕪爲蔓菁菜，誤矣。

【集解】弘景曰︰一種極似羊蹄而味酸，呼爲酸模，根亦療疥也。

大明曰︰所在有之，生山岡上。狀似羊蹄葉而小黃。莖葉俱細。節間生子，若茺蔚子。

藏器曰︰即是山大黃，一名當藥。其葉酸美，人亦采食其英。《爾雅》︰須，薞蕪。郭璞注云︰似羊蹄而稍細，味酸可食。一名蓚也。

時珍曰︰平地亦有。根葉花形並同羊蹄，但葉小味酸爲異。其根赤黃色。連根葉取汁煉霜，可製雄、汞。

【氣味】酸，寒，無毒。時珍曰︰葉酸，根微苦。

【主治】暴熱腹脹，生搗汁服，當下利。殺皮膚小蟲（藏器）。治疥（弘景）。療痢乃佳（保升）。去汗斑，同紫萍搗擦，數日即沒（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Nomenclature**
Alternate names: Shān yángtí [Mountain Sheep's-Hoof] (Bencao Gangmu); Shān dàhuáng [Mountain Rhubarb] (Shiyi); Sūnwú (Erya); Suānmǔ [Sour Mother] (Bencao Gangmu); Xiào (same source); Dāngyào [Appropriate Medicine].

Li Shizhen remarks: 'Sūnwú' is a phonetic transformation of 'suānmó' [Sorrel], and 'suānmó' is in turn a transformation of 'suānmǔ'; all these names derive from the sour taste of the plant. It shares a name with the three-leaved wood sorrel [Oxalis]. Zhang Yuxi's identification of sūnwú with turnip greens [mángjīng cài] was an error.

**Collected Commentaries**
Tao Hongjing states: There is a variety that closely resembles yángtí [Rumex japonicus] but tastes sour; it is called suānmó. Its root also treats scabies.

Da Ming states: It is found everywhere, growing on mountain ridges. Its appearance resembles the leaves of yángtí but is smaller and somewhat yellow. Both stem and leaves are slender. Seeds grow between the nodes, resembling those of chōngwèi [Leonurus].

Chen Cangqi states: This is precisely shān dàhuáng [Mountain Rhubarb], also called dāngyào. Its leaves are pleasantly sour, and people also gather and eat its young shoots. The Erya records: 'Xū is sūnwú.' Guo Pu's commentary notes: 'It resembles yángtí but is somewhat more slender; the flavor is sour and edible. Another name is xiào.'

Li Shizhen states: It is also found on flat ground. The root, leaves, and flower form are all the same as yángtí, differing only in that the leaves are smaller and the flavor is sour. Its root is reddish-yellow in color. Juice extracted from the whole plant — root and leaves together — can be reduced into a frost [crystalline extract], which may be used to process realgar [xiong, arsenic sulfide] and mercury [hóng, quicksilver].

**Flavor and Nature**
Sour; cold; non-toxic. Li Shizhen adds: the leaves are sour; the root is mildly bitter.

**Principal Treatments**
- Acute febrile abdominal distension: pound fresh plant, extract juice, and take orally — this should induce purgation. Also kills small skin parasites [insects]. (Chen Cangqi)
- Treats scabies. (Tao Hongjing)
- Effective for treating dysentery. (Bao Sheng)
- Removes sweat-stains [pityriasis versicolor / tinea versicolor]: pound together with zǐpíng [Spirodela / purple duckweed] and rub onto the affected area; the spots disappear within several days. (Li Shizhen)

results.translator_notes: 1. The passage notes that the crystalline extract ('frost') produced by reducing the juice of suānmó can be used to 'process' (製) realgar and mercury — likely referring to alchemical or pharmaceutical refinement procedures common in Ming-era materia medica. 2. 'Sweat stains' (汗斑) most plausibly corresponds to tinea versicolor (pityriasis versicolor), a fungal skin condition causing discolored patches; the topical remedy with duckweed is notable. 3. Zhang Yuxi (掌禹錫), the Song-dynasty pharmacologist, is explicitly critiqued for misidentifying sūnwú as a Brassica vegetable (turnip/mustard greens). 4. The Erya quotation and Guo Pu's gloss are accurately cited and consistent with received texts.

results.translation:
**[Collected Explanations]** Li Shizhen states: Dragon's Tongue [Longshe] grows in ponds, marshes, lakes, and waterways of the southern regions. Its leaves resemble those of large-leafed Chinese cabbage [song cai] and plantain [fu yi, Plantago]. The root grows at the bottom of the water, sending up a stem that rises above the surface, where it bears white flowers. The root resembles that of the carrot [hu luobo] and is fragrant. Pounding it to extract the juice can soften [the shells of] goose and duck eggs. Alchemists and formulary practitioners [fang jia] use it to process cinnabar [dan sha], calcine alum [bai fan], and subdue the Three Yellows [san huang: realgar, orpiment, and sulfur].

**[Flavor and Nature]** Sweet and salty; cold; non-toxic.

**[Principal Indications]** Carbuncles and deep abscesses [yong ju]; scalds and burns from boiling water or fire. Pound the fresh herb and apply topically. (Li Shizhen)

entities_mentioned.name: 龍舌草 (Longshe Cao / Dragon's Tongue Herb)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ottelia alismoides (Family Hydrocharitaceae) — a common aquatic herb of southern China, also sometimes identified with related species such as Sagittaria or Alisma, but Ottelia alismoides is the most widely accepted identification
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — aquatic habitat, large leaves resembling cabbage or plantain, white flowers, and fragrant carrot-like roots — is consistent with Ottelia alismoides, though some scholars have suggested Alisma plantago-aquatica.

entities_mentioned.name: 菘菜 (Song Cai / Chinese Cabbage)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis or related Brassica species
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for leaf shape.

entities_mentioned.name: 芣苡 (Fu Yi / Plantain)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago asiatica or Plantago major
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for leaf shape.

entities_mentioned.name: 胡蘿蔔 (Hu Luobo / Carrot)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Daucus carota subsp. sativus
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator for the root form and fragrance of the Dragon's Tongue root.

entities_mentioned.name: 丹砂 (Dan Sha / Cinnabar)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mercury sulfide (HgS)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in alchemical/pharmaceutical processing, with Dragon's Tongue herb employed as a processing agent.

entities_mentioned.name: 白礬 (Bai Fan / Alum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Potassium alum (KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O)
entities_mentioned.notes: Calcined with the herb in alchemical preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: 三黃 (San Huang / The Three Yellows)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Realgar (As₄S₄), orpiment (As₂S₃), and sulfur (S) — the standard triad in classical Chinese alchemy
entities_mentioned.notes: The exact substances grouped as 'Three Yellows' varied by context; in alchemical use this triad is most common, but in pharmaceutical contexts it can refer to Rheum, Coptis, and Scutellaria.

results.translator_notes: The phrase 方家 (fang jia) refers to practitioners of alchemical or technical formulary arts, not physicians per se — translated as 'alchemists and formulary practitioners' to preserve this nuance. The claim that the plant's juice can soften goose and duck eggshells is notable as a folk observation of enzymatic or acidic activity. The medicinal use — pounding fresh herb and applying topically to carbuncles and burns — is a straightforward external application with no internal dosage described. The plant's cold nature and salty-sweet flavor are consistent with its classical categorization as a heat-clearing, toxin-resolving herb.

【釋名】水菖（《別錄》）、水宿（《別錄》）、莖蒲（《別錄》）、菖陽（《拾遺》）、溪蓀（《拾遺》）、蘭蓀（弘景）。

時珍曰︰此即今池澤所生菖蒲，葉無劍脊，根肥白而節疏慢，故謂之白菖。古人以根爲菹食，謂之菖本，亦曰菖，文王好食之。其生溪澗者，名溪蓀。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰白菖十月採。

藏器曰︰即今之溪蓀也。一名菖陽。生水畔。人亦呼爲菖蒲。與石上菖蒲都別。根大而臭，色正白。

頌曰︰水菖蒲，生溪澗水澤中甚多，失水則枯。葉似石菖，但中心無脊。其根幹後，輕虛多滓，不堪入藥。

時珍曰︰此有二種︰一種根大而肥白節疏者，白菖也，俗謂之泥菖蒲；一種根瘦而赤節稍密者，溪蓀也，俗謂之水菖蒲。葉俱無劍脊。溪蓀氣味勝似白菖，並可殺蟲，不堪服食。

【氣味】甘，無毒。

《別錄》曰︰甘、辛，溫。汁製雄黃、雌黃、砒石。

【主治】食諸蟲（《別錄》）。主風濕咳逆，去蟲，斷蚤虱（弘景）。研末，油調，塗疥瘙（蘇頌）。
results.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**
Alternate names: Shuǐ Chāng [Water Calamus] (Bielu), Shuǐ Sù [Water Lodge] (Bielu), Jīng Pú [Stem Cattail] (Bielu), Chāng Yáng (Shiyi), Xī Sūn [Stream Iris] (Shiyi), Lán Sūn (Hongjing).

Li Shizhen states: This is the calamus [菖蒲, Acorus] that grows in present-day ponds and marshes. Its leaves lack the sword-like midrib, and its roots are fat, white, and sparsely jointed — hence it is called Bái Chāng [White Calamus]. The ancients pickled its roots to eat as a condiment, calling it chāng běn or also chāng [菖], and King Wen [of Zhou] was fond of eating it. The variety growing in streams and mountain brooks is called Xī Sūn.

**Collected Commentaries**
The Bielu states: White Calamus is harvested in the tenth month.

Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: This is what is now called Xī Sūn. It is also named Chāng Yáng. It grows along the water's edge. People also call it Chāng Pú [calamus/sweet flag]. It is entirely distinct from the rock-growing Shí Chāng Pú [stone calamus]. The roots are large and malodorous, and pure white in color.

Su Song [Song] states: Water Calamus grows abundantly in streams, brooks, and marshes, and withers when removed from water. Its leaves resemble those of Stone Calamus but lack a midrib down the center. After drying, the roots become light, spongy, and full of dregs, and are not suitable for medicinal use.

Li Shizhen states: There are two varieties of this plant. One has large, fat, white roots with sparse joints — this is Bái Chāng [White Calamus], commonly called Ní Chāng Pú [Mud Calamus]. The other has slender, reddish roots with somewhat denser joints — this is Xī Sūn, commonly called Shuǐ Chāng Pú [Water Calamus]. Neither variety has the sword-like midrib in its leaves. Xī Sūn surpasses Bái Chāng in aroma and flavor; both can kill parasites/insects, though neither is suitable for ingestion as food or medicine.

**Flavor and Nature**
Sweet; non-toxic.

The Bielu states: Sweet, acrid [pungent], and warm. The juice can be used to process [neutralize or prepare] realgar [雄黃, arsenic disulfide], orpiment [雌黃, arsenic trisulfide], and arsenic [砒石, arsenolite].

**Indications and Treatments**
- Kills and expels intestinal worms and parasites when eaten. (Bielu)
- Treats wind-dampness [fengshi] with cough and counterflow [qi rebellion]; expels worms; eliminates fleas and lice. (Hongjing [Tao Hongjing])
- Ground into powder, mixed with oil, and applied topically to treat scabies and itching sores. (Su Song)

entities_mentioned.name: 白菖 (Bái Chāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acorus calamus L. (Sweet Flag / Calamus) — the large, white-rooted, sparsely-jointed marsh variety
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen distinguishes this from the medicinally preferred Shi Chang Pu (Acorus gramineus) and identifies two sub-varieties: Bái Chāng (mud calamus, large white roots) and Xī Sūn (stream calamus, slender reddish roots). The text notes the roots are unsuitable for internal medicine use due to poor quality after drying.

entities_mentioned.name: 溪蓀 (Xī Sūn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Acorus calamus var. or Iris pseudacorus / a related wetland Acorus; some sources identify it as Iris sanguinea or a stream-dwelling Acorus variant
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a slimmer, reddish-rooted, more densely jointed relative of Bái Chāng, growing in streams. Its superior aroma relative to Bái Chāng is noted, but internal use is still discouraged.

entities_mentioned.name: 石上菖蒲 (Shí Shàng Chāng Pú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acorus gramineus Soland. (Stone Calamus / Dwarf Sweet Flag)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a distinct and superior species growing on rocks, explicitly contrasted with White Calamus. The preferred medicinal form of acorus in classical Chinese medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 雄黃 (Xióng Huáng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Realgar (arsenic disulfide, As₂S₂)
entities_mentioned.notes: The juice of White Calamus is noted as a processing agent for this mineral substance.

entities_mentioned.name: 雌黃 (Cí Huáng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Orpiment (arsenic trisulfide, As₂S₃)
entities_mentioned.notes: Another mineral processed using the juice of White Calamus.

entities_mentioned.name: 砒石 (Pī Shí)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arsenolite (arsenic trioxide, As₂O₃)
entities_mentioned.notes: A highly toxic arsenic mineral also processed with White Calamus juice.

The text mentions 'chāng 菖' with an apparent lacuna or OCR issue in '亦曰菖' — the second character after 菖 appears truncated in the source; this is retained as-is with the assumption that a character was lost in transmission. 2. 'Food諸蟲' in the 主治 section is rendered as 'kills and expels intestinal worms when eaten' following the standard interpretation of 食諸蟲 as ingesting the plant to treat internal parasites. 3. Li Shizhen's remark that 溪蓀 is 'not suitable for ingestion' (不堪服食) may reflect concerns about toxicity or simply poor palatability/efficacy compared to stone calamus. 4. The attribution of King Wen of Zhou (文王) eating calamus root as a preserved food is a well-known classical allusion and is translated accordingly. 5. 汁製 is translated as 'process/prepare' in the pharmaceutical sense — the juice was used as a medium to modify the properties of toxic minerals, a classical pharmaceutical technique.

results.original_text:
【釋名】甘蒲（蘇恭）、醮石（《吳普》），花上黃粉名蒲黃。

恭曰︰香蒲即甘蒲，可作薦者。春初生，取白爲菹，亦堪蒸食。山南人謂之香蒲，以菖蒲爲臭蒲也。蒲黃即此蒲之花也。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰香蒲生南海池澤。蒲黃生河東池澤，四月採之。

頌曰︰香蒲，蒲黃苗也。處處有之，以泰州者爲良。春初生嫩葉，未出水時，紅白色茸茸然。取其中心入地白蒻，大如匕柄者，生啖之，甘脆。又以醋浸，如食筍，大美。《周禮》謂之蒲菹，今人罕有食之者。至夏抽梗於叢葉中，花抱梗端，如武士棒杵，故俚俗謂之蒲槌，亦曰蒲厘花。其蒲黃，即花中蕊屑也。細若金粉，當欲開時便取之。市𠪨以蜜搜作果食貨賣。

時珍曰︰蒲叢生水際，似莞而褊，有脊而柔，二、三月苗。采其嫩根，瀹過作葅，一宿可食。亦可炸食、蒸食及晒乾磨粉作餅食。《詩》云︰其蔌伊何？惟筍及蒲。是矣。八、九月收葉以爲席，亦可作扇，軟滑而溫。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。時珍曰︰寒。

【主治】五臟心下邪氣，口中爛臭，堅齒明目聰耳。久服輕身耐老（《本經》）。去熱燥，利小便（寧原）。生啖，止消渴（汪穎）。補中益氣，和血脈（《正要》）。搗汁服，治妊婦勞熱煩躁，胎動下血（時珍。出《產乳》）。
results.translation:
**[Nomenclature]** Also known as Gan Pu [Sweet Bulrush] (Su Gong), and Jiao Shi (Wu Pu). The yellow powder found on its flowers is called Pu Huang [Cattail Pollen].

Su Gong states: Xiang Pu is the same as Gan Pu; it is suitable for making rush mats. It sprouts in early spring, and its white inner shoots are gathered to make pickles [ju/菹]; they may also be steamed and eaten. The people of Shannan call it Xiang Pu [Fragrant Bulrush], distinguishing it from Acorus [Shi Chang Pu], which they call Chou Pu [Stinking Bulrush]. Pu Huang is simply the flower of this bulrush.

**[Collected Commentaries]** The Bielu states: Xiang Pu grows in pools and marshes of Nanhai. Pu Huang grows in pools and marshes of Hedong; it is gathered in the fourth month.

Su Song states: Xiang Pu is the parent plant of Pu Huang. It is found everywhere, with the finest specimens coming from Taizhou. In early spring, tender shoots emerge; before they have risen above the water, they are covered with soft red-and-white down. The white inner pith at the base, as thick as the handle of a ladle, may be eaten raw — it is sweet and crisp. It can also be steeped in vinegar and eaten like bamboo shoots, with excellent flavor. The Rites of Zhou [Zhouli] refers to this as Pu Ju [pickled bulrush]. Today, few people still eat it. By summer, stalks extend upward from among the clustered leaves, with the flower spike clasped at the tip of each stalk, resembling a soldier's club or pestle; hence commoners call it Pu Chui [Bulrush Hammer], also known as Pu Li Hua. Pu Huang is the fine staminal dust within the flowers — as fine as gold powder. It should be collected just as the flowers are about to open. Traders mix it with honey to make confections for sale.

Li Shizhen states: Bulrush grows in clusters at the water's edge. It resembles round rush [Scirpus] but is flatter, with a distinct midrib yet pliable. Shoots emerge in the second and third months. Its tender roots may be blanched and pickled overnight and eaten the next day. They may also be deep-fried, steamed, or dried and ground into flour to make cakes. The Book of Odes [Shi Jing] says: 'What are these vegetable offerings? Only bamboo shoots and bulrush.' This refers to the same plant. In the eighth and ninth months, the leaves are harvested for weaving into mats; they can also be fashioned into fans, being soft, smooth, and warm.

**[Qi and Flavor]** Sweet, neutral [ping], non-toxic. Li Shizhen adds: Cold.

**[Medicinal Actions and Indications]**
- Eliminates pathogenic qi [evil qi] lodged in the five viscera and below the heart; treats foul ulceration of the mouth; strengthens the teeth, brightens the eyes, and sharpens the hearing. Long-term use lightens the body and resists aging. (Shennong Bencao Jing)
- Clears heat and dryness; promotes urination. (Ning Yuan)
- Eaten raw, it alleviates wasting-thirst [xiao ke / diabetes-like condition]. (Wang Ying)
- Supplements the middle [jiao] and augments qi; harmonizes the blood and vessels. (Yinshan Zhengyao)
- The juice, taken as a drink, treats labor-heat [exhaustion fever], restlessness, and fetal stirring with bleeding in pregnant women. (Li Shizhen, citing Chanlü [On Childbirth])

entities_mentioned.name: 香蒲 (Xiang Pu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Typha orientalis or Typha latifolia (common cattail / bulrush)
entities_mentioned.notes: The whole plant; shoots and roots used as food. Also called Gan Pu and Jiao Shi.

entities_mentioned.name: 蒲黃 (Pu Huang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Typha orientalis pollen — the fine yellow pollen of cattail flowers
entities_mentioned.notes: Used medicinally and sold mixed with honey as a confection. Collected just before flowers fully open.

entities_mentioned.name: 菖蒲 (Chang Pu /臭蒲 Chou Pu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acorus calamus (sweet flag)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned only for contrast; called 'stinking bulrush' by the people of Shannan to distinguish it from Xiang Pu.

results.translator_notes: 1. The character 蒻 (ruò, meaning tender inner shoot or pith) in '白蒻' was rendered as 白 [pith/inner shoot] in the source; it has been interpreted and restored as 白蒻 based on context. 2. '市𠪨' contains a rare/possibly corrupt character (𠪨); interpreted as '市賈' or 'market traders' based on context. 3. The Shijing quotation '其蔌伊芳何' — the character 芳 may be an interpolation or OCR variant; the canonical text reads '其蔌維何' (Daya,韓奕); translated from the cleaned text. 4. 'Xiao ke' (消渴) is the classical Chinese term for a condition corresponding broadly to diabetes mellitus or severe thirst disorders. 5. 'Chanlü' (產乳) is cited as the source for the final pregnancy indication; this likely refers to a classical obstetric text. 6. Preparation methods extracted: (a) raw consumption of inner pith shoots; (b) vinegar pickling; (c) blanching and overnight pickling; (d) deep-frying; (e) steaming; (f) drying and grinding into flour for cakes; (g) pollen mixed with honey for confections; (h) juice extraction for medicinal drink.

results.title_inferred: Cattail Pollen (Puhuang) — Flavor, Indications, Preparations, and Discoveries
results.original_text:
【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】心腹膀胱寒熱，利小便，止血，消瘀血。久服輕身益氣力，延年神仙（《本經》）。治痢血，鼻衄吐血，尿血瀉血，利水道，通經脈，止女子崩中（甄權）。婦人帶下，月候不勻，血氣心腹痛，妊婦下血墜胎，血運血症，兒枕急痛，顛撲血悶，排膿，瘡癤游風腫毒，下乳汁，止泄精（大明）。涼血活血，止心腹諸痛（時珍）。
炒用澀腸，止瀉血、血痢，妙（大明）。

【發明】弘景曰：蒲黃，即蒲厘花上黃粉也。甚療血。《仙經》亦用之。

宗奭曰：汴人初得，羅去滓，以水調爲膏，擘爲塊。人多食之，以解心臟虛熱，小兒尤嗜之。過月則燥，色味皆淡，須蜜水和。不可多食，令人自利，極能虛人。

時珍曰：蒲黃，手足厥陰血分藥也，故能治血治痛。生則能行，熟則能止。與五靈脂同用，能治一切心腹諸痛，詳見禽部寒號蟲下。按許叔微《本事方》云：有士人妻舌忽脹滿口，不能出聲。一老叟教以蒲黃頻摻，比曉乃愈。又《芝隱方》云：宋度宗欲賞花，一夜忽舌腫滿口。蔡御醫用蒲黃、乾薑末等分，乾搽而愈。據此二說，則蒲黃之涼血活血可証矣。蓋舌乃心之外候，而手厥陰相火乃心之臣使，得乾薑是陰陽相濟也。
results.translation:
**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet, neutral [ping], non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:**
For cold and heat [conditions] in the heart, abdomen, and bladder; promotes urination; stops bleeding; disperses blood stasis. Long-term use lightens the body, strengthens vital energy [qi], prolongs life, and confers the vitality of an immortal (*Bencao Jing* [Divine Husbandman's Classic]).
Treats bloody dysentery, nosebleed, vomiting of blood, blood in the urine, bloody diarrhea; promotes the water passages; unblocks the channels and vessels; arrests uterine flooding [beng zhong] in women (Zhen Quan).
For vaginal discharge in women, irregular menstruation, pain from blood and qi in the heart and abdomen, uterine bleeding and threatened miscarriage in pregnant women, blood syncope and blood stasis conditions, postpartum abdominal acute pain [er zhen ji tong], blood-induced stupor from falls and blows, expelling pus, sores, boils, migratory wind-swelling toxins [you feng zhong du], promoting lactation, and stopping seminal emission (Daming).
Cools the blood and invigorates blood circulation; stops all pain in the heart and abdomen (Shizhen).
When stir-fried, it is astringent to the intestines and stops bloody diarrhea and blood dysentery — excellent (Daming).

**Elaborations and Discoveries:**
Tao Hongjing states: Puhuang is the yellow pollen on the flowers of the cattail [Typha]. It is highly effective for blood disorders. The *Immortals' Classic* [Xian Jing] also makes use of it.

Kou Zongshi states: When the people of Bian [Kaifeng] first obtain it, they sieve out the dregs, mix it with water into a paste, and press it into cakes. Many people eat it to relieve deficiency-heat of the heart; children are especially fond of it. After more than a month it dries out, its color and flavor both becoming faint, and it must then be mixed with honey-water. One should not eat too much of it — it loosens the bowels and can readily debilitate a person.

Li Shizhen states: Puhuang is a drug acting on the blood aspect [xue fen] of the hand and foot jueyin [Pericardium and Liver channels], and it is for this reason that it can treat blood disorders and relieve pain. In its raw state it can move [blood]; when processed [cooked/stir-fried] it can stop [bleeding]. Used together with Wulingzhi [Trogopterus dung], it can treat all pain in the heart and abdomen — details are given in the entry on the Cold-Calling Insect [han hao chong] in the Avian section.

Furthermore, Xu Shuwei's *Benshifang* records: A scholar's wife suddenly had her tongue swell to fill her entire mouth, and she was unable to make any sound. An elderly man advised frequent application of Puhuang to the tongue, and by dawn she had recovered. Again, the *Zhiyinfang* records: Song Emperor Duzong wished to view the flowers in the garden, and one night his tongue suddenly swelled to fill his mouth. Imperial Physician Cai used equal parts of Puhuang and dried ginger powder, applied dry, and he was cured. Based on these two accounts, the ability of Puhuang to cool the blood and invigorate blood circulation is well demonstrated. The tongue is the outward manifestation [hou] of the heart, and the ministerial fire [xiang huo] of the hand jueyin is the servant and emissary of the heart; the addition of dried ginger represents the mutual balancing of yin and yang.

entities_mentioned.name: 蒲黃 (Puhuang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Typha orientalis or Typha angustifolia (cattail pollen)
entities_mentioned.notes: The yellow pollen harvested from the flower spikes of cattail (Typha spp.). Used raw to move blood and stir-fried to stop bleeding. Still used in Traditional Chinese Medicine today.

entities_mentioned.name: 五靈脂 (Wulingzhi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trogopterus xanthipes feces (flying squirrel droppings)
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with Puhuang in the classical formula Shixiao San (Sudden Smile Powder) for blood stasis pain. Li Shizhen refers the reader to its entry under the 'Avian' section (禽部) under han hao chong.

entities_mentioned.name: 乾薑 (Ganjiang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale (dried ginger rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in equal parts with Puhuang as a dry topical application for tongue swelling. Li Shizhen explains this as an yin-yang balancing combination with Puhuang.

entities_mentioned.name: 《本經》(Bencao Jing)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Shennong Bencao Jing (Divine Husbandman's Classic of Materia Medica)
entities_mentioned.notes: Canonical early Chinese pharmacopoeia, attributed to the legendary Shennong.

entities_mentioned.name: 甄權 (Zhen Quan)
entities_mentioned.notes: Tang dynasty physician and pharmacologist, author of Yaoxing Lun (Discourse on Drug Properties). Cited as a secondary authority here.

entities_mentioned.name: 大明 (Daming)
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers to Ri Huazi (Sun Simiao's disciple's school) or the compiler of Rihuazi Bencao; 'Daming' is a conventional attribution in Bencao Gangmu for this source.

entities_mentioned.name: 許叔微 (Xu Shuwei) / 《本事方》(Benshifang)
entities_mentioned.notes: Xu Shuwei (1079–1154), Song dynasty physician; Benshifang (Formulae from Personal Experience) is his clinical case record compendium.

entities_mentioned.name: 宋度宗 (Song Emperor Duzong)
entities_mentioned.notes: Emperor Duzong of the Southern Song dynasty (r. 1264–1274). Mentioned as a historical patient in an anecdote about tongue swelling treated with Puhuang.

entities_mentioned.name: 《芝隱方》(Zhiyinfang)
entities_mentioned.notes: A Song dynasty formula collection cited by Li Shizhen; exact authorship uncertain.

results.translator_notes:
1. 'Er zhen ji tong' (兒枕急痛) literally means 'child's pillow acute pain,' a classical term for postpartum uterine cramping caused by retained blood clots pressing on the uterus.
2. 'You feng zhong du' (游風腫毒) refers to migratory erysipelas-like swelling and toxic sores.
3. Li Shizhen's statement that Puhuang acts on the 'hand and foot jueyin' channels refers to the Pericardium (手厥陰) and Liver (足厥陰) channels in Chinese medicine theory, both associated with blood regulation.
4. The formula combining Puhuang and Wulingzhi is the classical 'Shixiao San' (失笑散, Sudden Smile Powder), though not named explicitly here.
5. 'Beng zhong' (崩中) is a classical gynecological term for abnormally heavy uterine bleeding (metrorrhagia/uterine flooding).
6. The text attributed to Tao Hongjing (弘景曰) is drawn from his commentary in the Mingyi Bielu or annotations to the Shennong Bencao Jing.
7. OCR/orthographic note: '証' (an alternate form of 證) has been retained as is; the intended meaning is 'demonstrated/proven.'

【釋名】茭草（《說文》）、蔣草。

時珍曰︰按︰許氏《說文》︰菰本作 ，從瓜諧聲也。有米謂之凋菰，已見穀部菰米下。江南人呼菰爲茭，以其根交結也。蔣義未詳。

【集解】保升曰︰菰根生水中，葉如蔗、荻，久則根盤而濃。夏月生菌堪啖，名菰菜。三年者，中心生白苔如藕狀，似小兒臂而白軟，中有黑脈，堪啖者，名菰首也。

藏器曰︰菰首小者，擘之內有黑灰如墨者，名烏郁，人亦食之。晉張翰思吳中 、菰，即此也。

頌曰︰菰根，江湖陂澤中皆有之。生水中，葉如蒲、葦輩，刈以秣馬甚肥。春末生白茅如筍，即菰菜也，又謂之茭白，生熟皆可啖，甜美。其中心如小兒臂者，名菰手。作菰首者，非矣。《爾雅》云︰出隧，蘧蔬。注云︰生菰草中，狀似土菌，江東人啖之，甜滑。即此也。故南方人至今謂菌爲菰，亦緣此義。其根亦如蘆根，冷利更甚。二浙下澤處，菰草最多。其根相結而生，久則並土浮於水上，彼人謂之菰葑。刈去其葉，便可耕蒔，又名葑田。其苗有莖梗者，謂之菰蔣草。至秋結實，乃雕胡米也。歲飢，人以當糧。

宗奭曰︰菰乃蒲類。河朔邊人，止以飼馬作薦。八月開花如葦。結青子，合粟爲粥食，甚濟飢。杜甫所謂「波漂菰米沉雲黑」者，是也。

【釋名】水粟（《綱目》）、水栗子。

時珍曰︰陳藏器《拾遺》︰萍蓬草，即今水粟也。其子如粟，如蓬子也。俗人呼水粟包，又云水栗子，言其根味也。或作水笠。

【集解】藏器曰︰萍蓬草，生南方池澤。葉大如荇。花亦黃，未開時狀如袋。其根如栗。

時珍曰︰水粟，三月出水。莖大如指。葉似荇葉而大，徑四、五寸，初生如荷葉。六、七月開黃花，結實狀如角黍，長二寸許，內有細子一包，如罌粟。澤農采之，洗擦去皮，蒸曝，舂取米，作粥飯食之。其根大如栗，亦如雞頭子根，儉年人亦食之，作藕香，味如栗子。昔楚王渡江得萍實，大如斗，赤如日，食之甜如蜜者，蓋此類也。若水萍，安得有實耶？三、四月採莖葉取汁，煮硫黃能拒火。又段公路《北戶錄》有睡蓮，亦此類也。其葉如荇而大。其花布葉數重，當夏晝開花，夜縮入水，晝復出也。

子

【氣味】甘，澀，平，無毒。
甘，寒，無毒。

【主治】助脾濃腸，令人不飢（時珍）。

根
煮食，補虛，益氣力。久食，不飢，濃腸胃（藏器）。
results.translation:
**Explanations of Names**
Also called Shuìsù [Water Millet] (Bencao Gangmu) and Shuìlìzǐ [Water Chestnut Seed].

Li Shizhen states: In Chen Cangqi's *Shiyi* [Supplement to the Materia Medica], 萍蓬草 (pínpéngcǎo) is identified as the plant now called 水粟 (shuìsù, "water millet"). Its seeds resemble millet and also resemble the seeds of the pénghāo plant. Common people call it 水粟包 ("water millet pod"), and also 水栗子 ("water chestnut"), referring to the flavor of its root. It is sometimes written as 水笠.

**Collected Commentaries**
Chen Cangqi states: 萍蓬草 grows in ponds and marshes of the southern regions. Its leaves are as large as those of the 荇 [marshwort / water fringe, *Nymphoides* sp.]. Its flowers are also yellow; before opening they are shaped like a bag [i.e., pouch-like]. Its root resembles a chestnut.

Li Shizhen states: The 水粟 emerges above water in the third month [of the lunar calendar]. Its stem is as thick as a finger. Its leaves resemble those of 荇 but are larger, four to five inches in diameter; when newly grown they look like lotus leaves. In the sixth and seventh months it produces yellow flowers, and forms fruits shaped like 角黍 [glutinous rice dumplings wrapped in leaves], approximately two inches long. Inside is a packet of small seeds resembling poppy seeds. Marsh farmers harvest these, wash and rub off the skin, steam and dry them in the sun, then pound them to obtain the grain, which is made into porridge or cooked rice. The root is as large as a chestnut and also resembles the root of 雞頭子 [foxnut/gorgon fruit, *Euryale ferox*]. In years of scarcity people also eat the root; it has the fragrance of lotus root and a flavor like chestnut. The ancient account of the King of Chu crossing the river and obtaining a 萍實 [spatterdock fruit], as large as a bushel measure, as red as the sun, and as sweet as honey when eaten, must refer to this type of plant. How could ordinary duckweed (*Lemna* sp.) produce such a fruit? In the third and fourth months, the stems and leaves may be gathered and their juice extracted; when used to boil sulfur [brimstone], it renders the sulfur resistant to fire. Furthermore, in Duan Gonglu's *Beihu Lu* [Record of the Northern Household], the 睡蓮 [water lily] is described — this also belongs to the same category. Its leaves are like those of 荇 but larger. Its flowers have multiple layers of petals; in summer they open during the day, retract beneath the water at night, and re-emerge the following day.

**Seeds**

*Flavor and Nature:* Sweet, astringent, balanced [平], non-toxic.
Sweet, cold, non-toxic.

*Principal Therapeutic Uses:* Aids the spleen and thickens the intestines; prevents hunger (Li Shizhen).

**Root**
When boiled and eaten, supplements deficiency and strengthens physical vitality. With prolonged consumption, one does not feel hungry, and the intestines and stomach are strengthened (Chen Cangqi).

entities_mentioned.name: 萍蓬草 (pínpéngcǎo) / 水粟 (shuìsù)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nuphar pumila or Nuphar sinensis (spatterdock / yellow water-lily, family Nymphaeaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen's detailed description — yellow flowers, pouch-like bud, large floating leaves resembling lotus, seeds resembling poppy seeds inside an angular fruit — strongly matches Nuphar species. The famous 'Pingshi' (萍實) anecdote from the Zuo Zhuan (左傳) is here reinterpreted by Li Shizhen as referring to this plant rather than common duckweed.

entities_mentioned.name: 荇 (xìng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nymphoides peltata (water fringe / yellow floating heart)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size comparison reference for the leaves of 萍蓬草.

entities_mentioned.name: 雞頭子 (jītóuzǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Euryale ferox (foxnut / gorgon plant)
entities_mentioned.notes: Root compared in size and appearance to the root of Euryale ferox.

entities_mentioned.name: 睡蓮 (shuìlián)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nymphaea tetragona or related Nymphaea species (water lily)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced from Duan Gonglu's Beihu Lu; the description of flowers opening by day and closing/retracting at night is characteristic of Nymphaea species.

entities_mentioned.name: 水萍 (shuìpíng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lemna sp. (duckweed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen uses this as a counterexample, arguing that common duckweed could not produce the large, sweet 萍實 described in historical records.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source text has a lacuna after 'its root resembles' (其根如) in the Cangqi commentary — the character or characters specifying what the root resembles (almost certainly 栗, 'chestnut') are missing or were cut off by OCR; I have supplied 栗 in the cleaned text and translation based on context from the Shizhen commentary directly below, where the root is explicitly compared to a chestnut (大如栗). 2. The dual flavor entries for the seeds (甘澀平 and 甘寒) likely reflect two different sources or editorial layers; both are preserved. 3. The famous 楚王萍實 anecdote originates in the Zuo Zhuan (Ai Gong 第十二年) and was long debated; Li Shizhen's reattribution of it to Nuphar rather than Lemna is a notable philological intervention. 4. The fire-retardant property ascribed to the juice of the stems/leaves boiled with sulfur is a notable alchemical/technical claim with no modern verification.

【釋名】鳧葵（《唐本》）、水葵（《馬融傳》）、水鏡草（《土宿本草》）、靨子菜（《野菜譜》）、金蓮子。接余。

時珍曰︰按《爾雅》云︰荇，接余也。其葉苻。則鳧葵當作苻葵，古文通用耳。或鳧喜食之，故稱鳧葵，亦通。其性滑如葵，其葉頗似荇，故曰葵，曰荇。《詩經》作荇，俗呼荇絲菜。池人謂之荇公須，淮人謂之靨子菜，江東謂之金蓮子。許氏《說文》謂之KT，音戀。《楚詞》謂之屏風，云︰「紫莖屏風文綠波」是矣。

【集解】恭曰︰鳧葵即荇菜也。生水中。

頌曰︰處處池澤有之。葉似蓴而莖澀，根甚長，花黃色。郭璞注《爾雅》云︰叢生水中。葉圓在莖端，長短隨水深淺。江東人食之。陸璣《詩疏》云︰荇莖白，而葉紫赤色，正圓，徑寸余，浮在水上。根在水底，大如釵股，上青下白，可以按酒。用苦酒浸其白莖，肥美。今人不食，醫方亦鮮用之。

時珍曰︰荇與蓴，一類二種也。並根連水底，葉浮水上。其葉似馬蹄而圓者，蓴也；葉似蓴而微尖長者，荇也。夏月俱開黃花，亦有白花者。結實大如棠梨，中有細子。按︰寧獻王《庚辛玉冊》云︰鳧葵，黃花者是荇菜，白花者是白蘋即水鏡草，一種泡子名水鱉。雖有數種，其用一也。其莖葉根花，並可伏硫、煮砂、製礬。此以花色分別蘋、荇，似亦未穩，詳見蘋下。

【氣味】甘，冷，無毒。

【主治】消渴，去熱淋，利小便（《唐本》）。搗汁服，療寒熱（《開寶》）。搗敷諸腫毒，火丹游腫（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**
Alternate names: Fú Kuí [Teal's Mallow] (Tang Bencao), Shuǐ Kuí [Water Mallow] (Biography of Ma Rong), Shuǐ Jìng Cǎo [Water Mirror Herb] (Tusù Bencao), Yǎn Zǐ Cài [Dimple Vegetable] (Yecai Pu), Jīn Lián Zǐ [Golden Lotus Seed]. Also known as Jiē Yú.

Li Shizhen remarks: According to the Erya, xìng is another name for jiē yú, and its leaf is called fú. Therefore, fú kuí ('teal mallow') should properly be written as fú kuí ('fú mallow'), the two characters being interchangeable in ancient writing. Alternatively, since teals (fú) enjoy eating it, calling it fú kuí ('teal's mallow') is also acceptable. Its texture is smooth like mallow (kuí), and its leaves resemble xìng, hence it is called both kuí and xìng. The Shijing [Book of Odes] writes it as xìng; it is commonly called xìng sī cài [xìng-thread vegetable]. Pond-dwellers call it xìng gōng xū; people of the Huai region call it yǎn zǐ cài; people of Jiangdong call it jīn lián zǐ [golden lotus seed]. Xu Shen's Shuowen Jiezi records it as [ILLEGIBLE: one character, reconstructed as a rare graph, pronounced 'liàn']. The Chuci [Songs of Chu] calls it píng fēng [screen/windbreak], as in the line: 'Purple-stemmed screen gracing the green-patterned waves.'

**Collected Explanations**
Su Gong states: Fú kuí is precisely xìng cài [water clover]. It grows in water.

Su Song states: It is found in ponds and marshes everywhere. The leaves resemble those of Brasenia [water shield] but the stems are rough; the roots are very long; the flowers are yellow. Guo Pu's commentary on the Erya states: It grows in clusters in water. The leaves are round and situated at the tips of the stems, their length varying with the depth of the water. People of Jiangdong eat it. Lu Ji's Shishu [Commentary on the Odes] states: The stems of xìng are white, while the leaves are purplish-red, perfectly round, more than an inch in diameter, floating on the water surface. The roots lie at the bottom, as thick as a hairpin shank, green above and white below; they can be used to accompany wine. Soaking the white stems in vinegar yields a rich, delicious preparation. People today do not eat it, and it is seldom used in medical prescriptions.

Li Shizhen states: Xìng and Brasenia (chún) are two varieties of the same type. Both have roots anchored at the bottom with leaves floating on the surface. The plant whose leaves resemble a horse's hoof and are round is chún [Brasenia]; the plant whose leaves resemble those of chún but are slightly more pointed and elongated is xìng. Both bloom with yellow flowers in summer, though white-flowered varieties also exist. They bear fruits about the size of a crabapple, containing fine seeds within. According to the Gengxin Yuce by Prince Ning Xian: among fú kuí, those with yellow flowers are xìng cài, while those with white flowers are bái píng — that is, shuǐ jìng cǎo [water mirror herb] — and one variety bearing bubble-like fruit is called shuǐ biē [water turtle plant]. Although there are several varieties, their medicinal uses are the same. The stems, leaves, roots, and flowers can all be used to subdue sulfur [伏硫], decoct cinnabar [煮砂], and process alum [製礬]. This classification of píng and xìng by flower colour seems somewhat unstable; see the entry on Píng for further details.

**Flavour and Nature**
Sweet; cold; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments**
- Eliminates wasting-thirst [消渴, xiāo kě; diabetes-like condition], clears heat strangury [熱淋], and promotes urination (Tang Bencao).
- Pound the plant and drink the juice to treat alternating chills and fever [寒熱] (Kaibao Bencao).
- Pound and apply topically to all manner of swellings and toxic sores, erysipelas [火丹, huǒ dān], and wandering swellings [游腫] (Li Shizhen).

entities_mentioned.name: 莕菜 / 荇菜 (xìng cài)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nymphoides peltata (yellow floating heart) or related Nymphoides spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The text describes floating, round-leaved aquatic plants with yellow (or occasionally white) flowers and roots anchored at the bottom. Nymphoides peltata is the standard modern identification. The text also conflates or distinguishes related species such as Brasenia schreberi (chún/water shield) and possibly Nymphoides indica (white-flowered).

entities_mentioned.name: 鳧葵 (fú kuí)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nymphoides peltata
entities_mentioned.notes: Synonym used in Tang Bencao.

entities_mentioned.name: 蓴 (chún)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brasenia schreberi (water shield)
entities_mentioned.notes: Compared and contrasted with xìng as a related but distinct aquatic species.

entities_mentioned.name: 白蘋 / 水鏡草 (bái píng / shuǐ jìng cǎo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nymphoides indica (white water snowflake) or Marsilea quadrifolia
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified here as the white-flowered variant of the fú kuí group. Li Shizhen expresses skepticism about this flower-colour-based distinction.

entities_mentioned.name: 水鱉 (shuǐ biē)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydrocharis dubia (frog's-bit) or possibly Nymphoides species with inflated fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a variety bearing 'bubble-like' (泡子) fruit; precise identification uncertain.

results.translator_notes:
1. The character rendered 'KT' in the source is an OCR failure for a rare or archaic graph noted in the Shuowen Jiezi, pronounced 'liàn'; it has been marked [ILLEGIBLE] in the translation as the precise character cannot be reconstructed with certainty from context alone.
2. The term 伏硫煮砂製礬 refers to alchemical/pharmaceutical processing techniques (subduing sulfur, decocting cinnabar, processing alum) common in Song–Ming materia medica; these indicate the plant's use as a moderating agent in mineral drug preparation.
3. 火丹 (huǒ dān, 'fire cinnabar') is a traditional diagnosis generally corresponding to erysipelas or acute inflammatory skin conditions.
4. The source text has a lacuna where 頌曰 compares the leaf to an unnamed plant ('葉似 而莖澀'); context and Li Shizhen's later clarification confirm the omitted word is 蓴 (Brasenia), silently restored in the cleaned-up original text.
5. Li Shizhen's note of skepticism about classifying píng and xìng by flower colour is notable as an early example of critical source evaluation within the Bencao Gangmu.

【釋名】茆（卯、柳二音）。水葵（《詩疏》）、露葵（《綱目》）、馬蹄草。

時珍曰︰ 字，本作 ，從純。純乃絲名，其莖似之故也。《齊民要術》云︰ 性純而易生。種以淺深爲候，水深則莖肥而葉少，水淺則莖瘦而葉多。其性逐水而滑，故謂之 菜，並得葵名。顏之推《家訓》云︰蔡朗父諱純，改 爲露葵。北人不知，以綠葵爲之。《詩》

云︰薄采其茆，即 也。或諱其名，謂之錦帶。

【集解】保升曰︰ 葉似鳧葵，浮在水上。采莖堪啖。花黃白色，子紫色。三月至八月，莖細如釵股，黃赤色，短長隨水深淺，名爲絲 ，味甜體軟。九月至十月漸粗硬。十一月萌在泥中，粗短，名瑰 ，味苦體澀。人惟取汁作羹，猶勝雜菜。

時珍曰︰ 生南方湖澤中，惟吳越人善食之。葉如荇菜而差圓，形似馬蹄。其莖紫色，大如箸，柔滑可羹。夏月開黃花。結實青紫色，大如棠梨，中有細子。春夏嫩莖未葉者名稚，稚者小也。葉稍舒長者名絲 ，其莖如絲也。至秋老則名葵 ，或作豬 ，言可飼豬也。又訛爲瑰 、黽 焉。余見鳧葵下。

【氣味】甘，寒，無毒。

藏器曰︰ 雖水草，而性熱擁。

詵曰︰ 雖冷補，熱食及多食亦擁氣不下，甚損人胃及齒，令人顏色惡，損毛發。和醋食，令人骨痿。

【釋名】時珍曰︰藻乃水草之有紋者，潔淨如澡浴，故謂之藻。

【集解】頌曰︰藻生水中，處處有之。《周南》詩云「於以采藻，於沼於沚，於彼行潦」是也。陸璣注云︰藻生水底，有二種︰一種葉如雞蘇，莖如箸，長四、五尺；一種葉如蓬蒿，莖如釵股，謂之聚藻。二藻皆可食，煮熟去腥氣，米麵糝蒸爲茹，甚滑美。荊揚人飢荒以當谷食。

藏器曰︰馬藻生水中，如馬齒相連。

時珍曰︰藻有二種，水中甚多。水藻，葉長二、三寸，兩兩對生，即馬藻也；聚藻，葉細如絲及魚鰓狀，節節連生，即水蘊也，俗名鰓草，又名牛尾蘊，是矣。《爾雅》云︰蘊，牛藻也。郭璞注云︰細葉蓬茸，如絲可愛，一節長數寸，長者二、三十節，即蘊也。二藻皆可食，入藥，以藻爲勝。《左傳》云︰蘋蘩蘊藻之菜，即此。

【氣味】甘，大寒，滑，無毒。

【主治】去暴熱熱痢，止渴，搗汁服之。小兒赤白游疹，火焱熱瘡，搗爛封之（藏器）。

【發明】思邈曰︰凡天下極冷，無過藻菜。但有患熱毒腫並丹毒者，取渠中藻菜切搗敷之，濃三分，乾即易，其效無比。
results.translation:
**Explanation of Names:** Li Shizhen states: Zao [algae] is a water plant with a patterned texture; it is as clean and pure as bathing [澡浴, 'zao yu'], hence it is called zao.

**Collected Commentaries:** Su Song states: Zao grows in water and is found everywhere. The 'Zhou Nan' ode [in the Book of Songs] says, 'To gather algae — in ponds, in pools, in flowing roadside waters' — this refers to it. Lu Ji's commentary notes: Algae grow on the bottom of water and are of two kinds. One kind has leaves resembling chicken-perilla [Marrubium/Lycopus], stems like chopsticks, and grows four to five feet long. The other kind has leaves resembling mugwort [Artemisia], with stems like hairpin prongs, and is called Ju-zao ['clustered algae']. Both types are edible; when boiled the fishy odor is removed, and they may be mixed with rice or flour and steamed as a vegetable dish — they are quite smooth and delicious. The people of Jing and Yang [regions] use them as grain food in times of famine.

Chen Cangqi states: Ma-zao ['horse algae'] grows in water, with joints resembling horse teeth strung together.

Li Shizhen states: Algae are of two kinds, both very abundant in water. Shui-zao ['water algae'] has leaves two to three inches long, growing in opposite pairs — this is the Ma-zao ['horse algae']. Ju-zao ['clustered algae'] has leaves as fine as silk thread, resembling fish gills in shape, connected node by node — this is the Shui-yun, commonly called Sai-cao ['gill grass'] or Niu-wei-yun ['ox-tail yun']. The Erya states: 'Yun is the ox-algae.' Guo Pu's commentary notes: Its fine leaves are fluffy and silky, lovely as threads; each node is several inches long, and longer specimens have twenty to thirty nodes — this is yun. Both kinds of algae are edible and may be used medicinally, though the [water] algae [zao] is superior for medicinal use. The Zuo Zhuan states: 'The vegetables of ping, fan, yun, and zao' — this refers to these plants.

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet, greatly cold [da han], slippery [hua], non-toxic.

**Primary Treatments:** Eliminates sudden-onset heat [bao re] and heat-dysentery [re li], quenches thirst — pound and take the juice. For red and white wandering rashes [you zhen] in children, and for fire-inflammation [huo yan] and hot sores — pound to a pulp and apply as a poultice (Chen Cangqi).

**Elaborations:** Sun Simiao states: Of all things under heaven that are supremely cold, nothing surpasses algae as a vegetable. For those suffering from heat-toxin swellings [re du zhong] and cinnabar toxin [dan du, i.e., erysipelas], take algae from a canal or ditch, chop and pound it, and apply as a poultice three fen thick; replace when it dries. The efficacy is incomparable.

entities_mentioned.name: 水藻 / 馬藻 (Shuizao / Mazao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Potamogeton spp. or Myriophyllum spicatum / possibly Hydrilla verticillata or related aquatic macrophytes; the 'Ma-zao' description with opposite paired leaves suggests Potamogeton or Elodea-like plants
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen describes leaves 2–3 inches long in opposite pairs; this morphology is consistent with Potamogeton malaianus or similar pondweeds.

entities_mentioned.name: 聚藻 / 水蘊 / 鰓草 / 牛尾蘊 (Ju-zao / Shui-yun / Sai-cao / Niu-wei-yun)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myriophyllum spicatum (Eurasian watermilfoil) or Hydrilla verticillata; the description of fine silk-like or gill-like leaves in whorls connected node by node strongly suggests Myriophyllum or Hydrilla
entities_mentioned.notes: The Erya gloss 'yun, ox-algae' and Guo Pu's description of feathery nodes several inches long with 20–30 nodes per plant aligns well with Myriophyllum spicatum or Ceratophyllum demersum.

entities_mentioned.name: 藻菜 (Zao-cai, algae as medicinal vegetable)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: General aquatic algae/macrophytes as above
entities_mentioned.notes: Sun Simiao (author of Qianjin Yaofang) recommends canal algae as a topical poultice for erysipelas (dan du) and heat-toxin swellings.

results.translator_notes: 1. The OCR rendered '於沼於 ' with a missing character; the standard text of the Shi Jing (Book of Songs, 'Zhou Nan: Cai Ping') reads '於沼於沚' (in ponds, in pools), so '沚' has been silently restored. Similarly, '蘊' in the Erya quotation appeared as a blank/special character and has been restored from context. 2. 'Dan du' (丹毒) is traditionally translated as 'cinnabar toxin' and corresponds clinically to erysipelas (a streptococcal skin infection). 3. Sun Simiao (思邈) is the Tang dynasty physician, author of the Beiji Qianjin Yaofang. 4. The preparation methods described include: (a) pounding fresh algae and drinking the juice for heat, dysentery, and thirst; (b) pounding to a pulp and applying topically for skin rashes and hot sores in children; (c) chopping and pounding canal algae into a thick poultice (three fen deep) for heat-toxin swellings and erysipelas, replaced when dry.
